input.prompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

input.initialPrompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about a .NET MAUI issue where the keyboard covers the bottom input bar on Android. They mention that `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` no longer works in .NET 10 (which would be the next version after .NET 9). This is a known change. In .NET 10 (and actually starting with Android 11+), `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` was deprecated by Android itself. The modern approach uses `WindowInsetsCompat` and edge-to-edge display. In .NET MAUI for .NET 10, the recommended approach is to use the `android:windowSoftInputMode="adjustResize"` in the AndroidManifest.xml, but more importantly, to handle keyboard insets properly using the new edge-to-edge APIs or by listening to window insets.
So the actual fix involves keeping `adjustResize` in the manifest while also implementing `ViewCompat.SetOnApplyWindowInsetsListener` to detect keyboard insets and adjust the layout padding dynamically in response. For .NET 10 MAUI specifically, since it enables edge-to-edge display by default on Android, the keyboard behavior has shifted—I need to either use the new `KeyboardInsetsBehavior` if available or manually handle the window insets to properly account for the IME. The real challenge with .NET 10 is that edge-to-edge rendering is now the default, so the old resize behavior no longer automatically adjusts the content area when the keyboard appears. I need to use the new inset handling system instead, leveraging `ViewCompat.SetOnApplyWindowInsetsListener` in the Android platform code to properly respond to keyboard visibility changes. Writing platform-specific handler... I'll go with the IME insets approach since it's the most reliable way to handle keyboard appearance in modern MAUI without disrupting the entire view layout.
